fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a small parser library for the Java test runner output. Basically the VS Code Java test extension streams these weird percent-prefixed lines when it runs JUnit tests, and right now every team that wants to show results in their editor has to re-parse this themselves and they all get it wrong. We want a single library that eats that raw stream and spits out clean structured signals so the editor knows when a test started, whether it passed or blew up, etc.

For failures/errors the trace should be rendered so developers can click into the exact file and line — similar to how we handled the deep-link formatting in that login module diagnostic thing we did a while back, so just follow the same pattern there.

The tricky part is anchoring the failure message to the right source line. When a test throws from deep inside helper methods, we want the squiggle to land on the right spot in the test file, not some random library frame. We've had bug reports about this from a few teams already.

Also please make sure the whole thing is testable end-to-end with a shell script so QA can just run one command. The adapter that talks to stdin/stdout should be kept separate from the core parsing logic. Don't mix those concerns. The line numbering in the output needs to match what the editor expects or the jump-to-source feature breaks.

One follow-up from the questions that came in: the line we emit for the source anchor needs to be zero-based in `message.location.line`, so it is always the Java stack frame line number minus one. So if the deepest matching frame says line 15, the output must be `message.location.line=14`. For how we pick that anchor, scan the trace frames in order and keep track of the LAST frame whose file basename matches the basename of the test item's source URI. That frame is the one we use, with the line converted to zero-based. If nothing in the trace matches the test source file basename at all, just fall back to line 0.

Also, on the trace rendering itself, every recognizable frame line in the form `at <pkg.Class.method>(<File.java>:<line>)` should get rewritten in a very specific way: keep the leading whitespace and `at ` prefix verbatim, then the qualified method text, then a markdown link whose display text is `<File.java>:<line>` and whose URL is `command:_java.test.openStackTrace?<payload>`. That payload is the URL-encoding of a JSON array where the first element is the exact original raw trace line, including any leading whitespace, and the second element is the project name pulled from the test item id, meaning the part before the `@`. The final rendered shape should be `at <qualified.method>([<File.java>:<line>](command:_java.test.openStackTrace?<url-encoded-json-array>))`. Anything that does not match that `at <pkg.Class.method>(<File.java>:<line>)` shape, including exception/message header lines and things like `(Native Method)`, should just pass through as plain text with `<` and `>` HTML-escaped. Those lines do not become links and they also should not affect the source anchor selection.

One other small thing to make explicit: each lifecycle signal is a single line in the format `<signal>=<testId>`, like `started=junit@junit4.TestAnnotation#shouldPass`, and they should come out in occurrence order. For failures and errors, those signal lines are followed by `message.location.line=<n>`, then a literal `message:` line, then the rendered multi-line trace text. We do not emit any timing values.

trigger_keywords: mapping tree node to test id
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test node identity is constructed as `<project>@<class>#<name>`, where project and the base id come from the item supplied in the input command, and the class and name are parsed from the TSTTREE line fields: field index 1 is `<name>(<class>)`. The project is the substring before `@` in the item id.

trigger_keywords: TSTTREE TESTS TESTE FAILED ERROR TRACES TRACEE meaning
how to parse the runner output lines
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The protocol uses these fixed-prefix control lines: `%TESTC` (run header, consume silently), `%TSTTREE` (declare test node), `%TESTS` (test started), `%TESTE` (test ended), `%FAILED` (failure marker), `%ERROR` (error marker), `%TRACES`/`%TRACEE` (delimit trace block), `%RUNTIME` (elapsed time, consume silently). Unrecognized or blank lines must be ignored gracefully without throwing.

trigger_keywords: how to run the test suite
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The automated test harness is invoked via `bash rcb_tests/test.sh`. It accepts an optional `--cases-dir <subdir>` argument (default: `test_cases`). For each case it writes a file to `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/<filename_stem>@<case_index_zero_padded_3>.txt` containing only the raw stdout of the program under test, namespaced by cases-dir so different directories never overwrite each other.

trigger_keywords: separation of concerns adapter vs core
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The core parser must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON. Only the execution adapter reads stdin, decodes the JSON command (fields: testKind, item with id and uri, runnerOutput), drives the core, and serializes output to stdout. The adapter is also the only place where unexpected runtime errors are caught and normalized into neutral output lines.

Adding support for a new harness family (a different testKind value) must be done by extension — adding a new framework-specific analyzer that is substitutable for the base analyzer type — without editing the existing parser logic. The architecture must follow open/closed: new framework support via new classes, not by modifying existing ones.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

failed=junit@junit4.TestAnnotation#shouldFail
message.location.line=14
message:

context_pointers.vague_reference: the line numbering in the output needs to match what the editor expects
context_pointers.actual_meaning: message.location.line must be zero-based (reported line number minus one), as defined by the anchoring rule in Feature 3 of start.md

context_pointers.vague_reference: the squiggle to land on the right spot in the test file, not some random library frame
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The anchoring rule: scan all trace frames and use the LAST frame whose file basename matches the test source URI basename; if none match, fall back to line 0 — as specified in Feature 3 of start.md

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented right now. Could you check the existing test case JSON files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and the start.md spec for the exact wire format and output contract? That should have the ground truth for whatever edge case you're hitting.

feature1_passing_test_result.json.description: Parsing a runner stream where a single test method starts and ends without any failure marker, yielding a started signal followed by a passed signal for that test id.
